question: A PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller is the backbone of feedback loops in AMO labs — laser frequency locks, temperature stabilisation, and intensity servos all use PID controllers. The three terms and their roles:

options: P (proportional): output $\propto$ error $e(t)$, sets loop gain, reduces but does not eliminate steady-state error; I (integral): output $\propto \int e\,dt$, eliminates steady-state offset, adds phase lag; D (derivative): output $\propto de/dt$, damps oscillations, amplifies noise
The P term eliminates steady-state error; the I term provides fast corrections; the D term sets the overall gain
PID controllers can only be implemented in analogue electronics, not digitally
Increasing all three gains simultaneously always improves lock performance
passive.explanation: PID transfer function: $G(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$. Loop stability: the Bode criterion requires gain $< 1$ at the phase-crossover frequency. Common tuning: Ziegler-Nichols (increase $K_p$ until oscillation, then set $K_i, K_d$ from the oscillation period). In laser locking: P sets capture range, I corrects drift, D is often omitted (adds noise). Integrator wind-up (integrator saturates when the error is large) is a common problem — add anti-windup clamping. Unity-gain bandwidth: the frequency at which $|G(j\omega)| = 1$; must be below the phase margin for stability.

active.prompt: Match each PID term to its Laplace-domain action and effect on the loop:

pairs.definition: Gain $K_p \cdot e(t)$; increases loop gain; reduces (but does not zero) steady-state error; can cause oscillation if too large
Integrates error over time; eliminates steady-state offset; adds $-90°$ phase lag; risk of integrator wind-up
Responds to rate of change of error; adds phase lead; damps ringing; amplifies high-frequency noise
Frequency at which loop gain = 1; must lie below phase crossover ($< 180°$ phase shift) for stability; sets correction speed

active.explanation: Practical laser locking: typical PDH servo has UGB $\sim 1$ MHz (limited by cavity pole and EOM bandwidth). For intensity stabilisation: AOM or EOM as actuator; servo UGB $\sim 100$ kHz. For current locking: diode current as actuator, UGB $\sim 1$ MHz. Phase margin $> 45°$ recommended for robust lock. Digital PID (FPGA-based, e.g., Koheron, Vescent D2-125): update rate $\sim 100$ MHz; 16-bit precision; easy parameter tuning.

question: A lock-in amplifier (LIA) extracts a signal at a known reference frequency from a noisy background, enabling phase-sensitive detection. The key principle and performance metric are:

options: The LIA multiplies the input signal by a reference $\cos(\omega_r t + \phi)$, then low-pass filters the result — only components at exactly $\omega_r$ survive the multiplication; the output is proportional to the signal amplitude at $\omega_r$; the noise bandwidth is set by the time constant $\tau$ of the low-pass filter
A lock-in amplifier uses a bandpass filter centred at the reference frequency to select the signal
Lock-in amplifiers only work with signals above 1 kHz reference frequency
The time constant $\tau$ must be shorter than the modulation period for the LIA to function
passive.explanation: Lock-in SNR advantage: $\text{SNR}_{\rm LIA} = \text{SNR}_{\rm direct} \cdot \sqrt{B_{\rm in} / B_{\rm LIA}}$ where $B_{\rm in}$ is the input noise bandwidth and $B_{\rm LIA} = 1/(4\tau)$ is the LIA bandwidth. Example: measuring a 1 nV signal at 10 kHz in 10 Hz detection bandwidth rejects noise from 1 Hz to 100 kHz — $10^4$ bandwidth ratio → 40 dB SNR improvement. Time constant: $\tau = 100$ ms → bandwidth $\sim 2.5$ Hz. Dual phase detection (X and Y channels): $X = V\cos\phi$, $Y = V\sin\phi$, $R = \sqrt{X^2+Y^2}$ (amplitude), $\theta = \arctan(Y/X)$ (phase). AMO applications: absorption spectroscopy SNR enhancement; PDH error signal demodulation; noise floor characterisation.

question: Electronic noise in AMO lab circuits — photodetector amplifiers, voltage references, servo circuits — limits signal-to-noise ratios. The fundamental noise sources and their frequency dependences:

options: Johnson-Nyquist (thermal) noise: $S_V = 4k_BT R$ (white, V$^2$/Hz); shot noise on photocurrent: $S_I = 2eI$ (white, A$^2$/Hz); $1/f$ (flicker) noise: $S \propto 1/f$ dominant at low frequency; all sources add in quadrature
Johnson noise depends on the signal frequency, not just the resistance and temperature
Shot noise is always smaller than Johnson noise in practical circuits
$1/f$ noise is only present in digital circuits, not analogue
passive.explanation: Johnson noise RMS voltage: $V_n = \sqrt{4k_BT R B}$. At $R = 1$ k$\Omega$, $T = 300$ K, $B = 1$ MHz: $V_n = 4$ nV/$\sqrt{\rm Hz}$ (typical op-amp noise floor). Shot noise on photocurrent: at $I = 1$ mA, $I_n = \sqrt{2eIB} = 18$ pA/$\sqrt{\rm Hz}$. Standard quantum limit (SQL): photon shot noise sets the fundamental sensitivity in homodyne detection. Above the shot-noise limit requires squeezed states of light. Op-amp voltage noise: typical $\sim 1{-}5$ nV/$\sqrt{\rm Hz}$ (e.g., LT1028: 0.9 nV/$\sqrt{\rm Hz}$). Ground loops: capacitively-coupled $50$ Hz/60 Hz pick-up — eliminate by single-point grounding and star-wiring topology.

question: Electro-optic modulators (EOMs) and acousto-optic modulators (AOMs) are the primary tools for controlling laser frequency, intensity, and phase. Key differences in operation and bandwidth:

options: EOM: electro-optic crystal changes refractive index with applied voltage; bandwidth DC to several GHz; no frequency shift; used for phase modulation/PDH sidebands/fast switching. AOM: sound wave creates a travelling diffraction grating; bandwidth $\sim 1{-}100$ MHz; shifts frequency by the RF drive frequency; used for frequency shifting, intensity control, and pulsing
AOMs can modulate at GHz frequencies; EOMs are limited to $< 1$ MHz
EOMs shift the laser frequency by the applied voltage; AOMs do not change the frequency
Both AOMs and EOMs require the laser beam to pass through a crystal at Brewster angle
passive.explanation: AOM parameters: diffraction efficiency 70–90% into first order; rise time $\sim d/v_s$ where $d$ is the beam diameter and $v_s \sim 4$ km/s (sound speed in TeO$_2$); at $d = 0.5$ mm, rise time $\sim 125$ ns. Centre frequency 40–400 MHz (most common: 80 MHz for Rb). Double-pass AOM: beam passes through twice, frequency shift doubles ($2\times$ RF), and pointing remains fixed when RF frequency is tuned. EOM usage: resonant EOMs have high sideband efficiency at a fixed modulation frequency; broadband EOMs allow arbitrary modulation. Half-wave voltage ($V_\pi$): $\sim 100$ V for bulk KDP at 780 nm; $\sim 10$ V for waveguide EOMs — high voltage op-amps or RF amplifiers required.

question: Photodetectors in AMO labs convert optical power to electrical signal. The main figures of merit for a photodiode transimpedance amplifier (TIA) circuit are:

options: Responsivity $\mathcal{R}$ (A/W); transimpedance gain $R_f$ ($\Omega$); bandwidth $f_{-3\rm dB} = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{R_f C_f})$ (set by feedback RC); noise-equivalent power NEP = $V_{n,\rm out}/(R_f\mathcal{R})$; unity-gain bandwidth of op-amp limits achievable $R_f \cdot f_{-3\rm dB}$ product
Responsivity is measured in V/W and is independent of the op-amp gain
Higher transimpedance gain always increases the bandwidth of the detector
The bandwidth of a TIA is limited only by the photodiode capacitance
passive.explanation: TIA design: $R_f$ sets gain, $C_f$ is a small feedback capacitor for stability ($C_f \sim 1$ pF for $R_f = 10$ k$\Omega$). Bandwidth: $f_{-3\rm dB} \approx \sqrt{\rm GBW}/(2\pi R_f C_d)^{1/2}$ where $C_d$ is diode capacitance. At $R_f = 10$ k$\Omega$ and Si photodiode $C_d = 10$ pF with GBW = 200 MHz: bandwidth $\sim 100$ kHz. Trade-off: $R_f \uparrow$ → more gain, more Johnson noise, lower bandwidth. Si photodiode: $\mathcal{R} \approx 0.5$ A/W at 780 nm (Rb). InGaAs: 1.0 A/W at 1560 nm. Balanced photodetection: subtract two photodiodes in balanced homodyne → common-mode noise (intensity fluctuations) cancelled to $\sim 40$ dB below shot noise.

active.prompt: Match each detector/amplifier parameter to its definition and typical value for an 780 nm Si photodetector:

pairs.definition: Photocurrent per optical power: $\mathcal{R} = \eta e\lambda/(hc)$; for Si at 780 nm: $\sim 0.5$ A/W (quantum efficiency $\eta \approx 80\%$)
Output voltage per photocurrent: $V_{\rm out}/I_{\rm pd} = R_f$; typical values 1 k$\Omega$–1 M$\Omega$ depending on signal level and bandwidth needed
Minimum detectable optical power per $\sqrt{\rm Hz}$; dominated by Johnson noise of $R_f$: NEP $= \sqrt{4k_BT/R_f}/\mathcal{R}$; at $R_f = 10$ k$\Omega$: $\sim 80$ pW/$\sqrt{\rm Hz}$
Maximum optical input before op-amp saturates: $P_{\rm sat} = V_{\rm supply}/(R_f \mathcal{R})$; at $R_f = 10$ k$\Omega$, $\pm 12$ V supply: $\sim 2.4$ mW

active.explanation: Practical tips: (1) Use a low-noise op-amp (e.g., OPA847, ADA4898) for high-bandwidth TIA. (2) Keep PCB traces from photodiode to TIA input as short as possible to minimise stray capacitance. (3) Shield the photodiode from stray light. (4) For single-photon detection: use avalanche photodiode (APD) in Geiger mode or SPAD — quantum efficiency 50–70% at 700–900 nm, dead time $\sim 50$ ns, dark count rate $\sim 100$ s$^{-1}$.

question: RF signal generation is essential for driving AOMs, EOMs, and ion trap RF electrodes. A direct digital synthesis (DDS) chip and a phase-locked loop (PLL) serve complementary roles:

options: DDS: numerically controlled oscillator with DAC; arbitrary frequency with fine resolution ($\Delta f \sim$ mHz); fast frequency switching ($< 1$ $\mu$s); limited output frequency ($< 500$ MHz); phase coherent switching. PLL: multiplies a reference (e.g., 10 MHz OCXO) to high frequency ($>$ GHz); low phase noise but slower tuning
DDS chips generate frequencies only by integer multiples of a fixed reference
PLLs cannot be used above 1 GHz because the phase detector fails at high frequencies
DDS and PLL cannot be combined in the same RF signal chain
passive.explanation: DDS example: AD9959 (4-channel, 500 MSPS, 32-bit frequency word) → frequency resolution $\sim 0.1$ Hz at 100 MHz. Phase noise: $-130$ dBc/Hz at 1 kHz offset (limited by DAC quantisation). Spurious free dynamic range (SFDR): $\sim 75$ dBc. Common usage: drive AOM at 80 MHz for detuning control, update frequency word every shot to sweep atom cloud. PLL example: HMC835 (25 MHz–3 GHz, fractional-N, $< -100$ dBc/Hz phase noise). Ion trap RF: typically 10–100 MHz at $\sim 10$ W — requires RF amplifier (e.g., Mini-Circuits ZHL series) and helical resonator to transform 50 $\Omega$ to trap impedance ($\sim$ k$\Omega$). Trap drive at $\Omega = 2\pi \times 25$ MHz needs $V_{\rm rf} \sim 100{-}1000$ V$_{\rm pk-pk}$ depending on trap geometry.

question: A helical resonator is used to impedance-match an RF amplifier (50 $\Omega$) to the high-impedance capacitive load of an ion trap electrode. The key design parameters are:

options: Quality factor $Q = \omega L/R_{\rm coil}$; resonant frequency $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ (where $C$ includes trap capacitance); voltage step-up ratio $\sim \sqrt{Q}$; loaded $Q$ reduced by the trap resistance; bandwidth $\Delta\omega = \omega_0/Q$
A helical resonator is a bandpass filter with no voltage amplification
The resonant frequency depends only on the trap electrode capacitance, not the coil
Increasing the coil resistance improves the voltage step-up ratio
passive.explanation: Helical resonator design: $Q \sim 200{-}500$ for a well-designed coil (silver or copper on Teflon former, inside copper can). Voltage gain: $V_{\rm trap}/V_{\rm in} = Q/2 \approx 100{-}250$ for $Q = 200{-}500$, so 10 W input ($\sim 45$ V$_{\rm pk}$) → trap voltage $\sim 4000{-}11000$ V$_{\rm pk}$. Trap capacitance: typically 5–50 pF; determines coil inductance $L = 1/(\omega_0^2 C)$. Tuning: movable copper slug inside coil core. Monitoring: pickup coil (one turn) near the main coil; capacitive divider on the electrode. Practical: thermally mount the coil can to the vacuum chamber for temperature stability of the drive frequency.
